The last review was completed 95 days ago, which exceeds the 90-day requirement specified in CM-7.5 RULE-03, making this scenario non-compliant with the authorized software review requirements.

scenarios.scenario-detail:
MedTech Innovations, a healthcare technology company with 6,800 employees developing FDA-regulated medical devices, operates a sophisticated patient data management platform processing sensitive health information across 22 medical facilities nationwide. The organization maintains comprehensive physical and environmental protection policies per PE-1 with designated officials managing policy development, annual review cycles, and complete dissemination to all personnel with physical security responsibilities, ensuring full compliance with healthcare regulations and industry standards. Supply chain risk assessments per RA-3.1 are conducted annually for all critical vendors with enhanced due diligence for high-risk suppliers, comprehensive evaluation of disruption risks, counterfeit components, malicious code insertion, and improper delivery practices, with documented integration into system risk management processes and additional security validation for components from high-risk geographic regions. The software development lifecycle implements SA-10.5 requirements with real-time integrity mapping between master build data and on-site master copies, automated cryptographic hash verification within 24-hour cycles, comprehensive documentation of all verification activities, and 48-hour resolution requirements for any detected discrepancies. Change management processes per CM-3.2 require comprehensive testing in production-equivalent environments, complete validation of security controls and functional requirements, detailed documentation with Change Control Board approval, and strict adherence to emergency change procedures with 72-hour post-implementation validation requirements. The organization's authorized software policy per CM-7.5 implements deny-all, permit-by-exception controls with quarterly software list reviews, comprehensive integrity verification using digital signatures and cryptographic checksums, detailed logging of execution attempts, and monthly exception reviews with security team approval. Privacy protection measures include comprehensive just-in-time notice implementation at all PII collection points with appropriate timing, interface consistency, and detailed logging of notice presentations. However, during a recent system upgrade involving high-risk data processing functionality, the just-in-time privacy notice was delayed by 8 seconds after user interaction initiation due to system performance optimization priorities.

Policies referenced: policy_CM-7.5, policy_CM-3.2, policy_PT-5.1, policy_PE-1, policy_RA-3.1, policy_SA-10.5
scenarios.non-compliant-reason: The just-in-time notice was presented 8 seconds after user interaction initiation for a high-risk data processing action, exceeding the required 5-second timeframe specified in PT-5.1 RULE-02. This violates the requirement that data actions with heightened privacy risk must trigger just-in-time notices within 5 seconds of user interaction initiation.

scenarios.scenario-detail:
CyberSecure Financial Services, a leading fintech company with 9,200 employees providing digital banking solutions to over 2.5 million customers, operates a mission-critical trading platform processing billions of dollars in transactions daily across global markets. The organization maintains exemplary physical and environmental protection policies per PE-1 with comprehensive documentation, designated policy officials, annual review cycles, and complete dissemination to all relevant personnel with clear roles and responsibilities defined for facility managers, security teams, and compliance officers. Privacy protection measures per PT-5.1 ensure just-in-time notices are presented at all PII collection points within required timeframes, with comprehensive logging, interface consistency, and regular updates when processing purposes change, maintaining full transparency with customers regarding data usage and processing activities. Supply chain risk assessments per RA-3.1 are conducted annually for all vendors with enhanced due diligence for high-risk suppliers, comprehensive evaluation of disruption risks, counterfeit components, malicious code insertion, and delivery practices, with documented integration into enterprise risk management processes and additional security validation for critical components from high-risk geographic regions. The authorized software policy per CM-7.5 implements comprehensive deny-all, permit-by-exception controls across all systems with quarterly software list reviews, detailed integrity verification using digital signatures and cryptographic checksums, comprehensive logging and monitoring of execution attempts, and monthly exception reviews with security team approval workflows. Development lifecycle processes incorporate SA-10.5 requirements with automated integrity mapping between master build data and on-site copies, cryptographic hash verification, comprehensive documentation of verification activities, and automated discrepancy detection with 48-hour resolution requirements for maintaining system integrity. During a recent emergency database configuration change to address a critical performance issue affecting customer transactions, the organization implemented the necessary modifications immediately to prevent service disruption, with plans to complete comprehensive testing and validation procedures. However, 96 hours have elapsed since the emergency change implementation without completion of the required post-implementation testing and validation procedures.

Policies referenced: policy_CM-7.5, policy_CM-3.2, policy_PT-5.1, policy_PE-1, policy_RA-3.1, policy_SA-10.5
scenarios.non-compliant-reason: The emergency change was implemented 96 hours ago without completing the required testing and validation procedures, exceeding the 72-hour requirement specified in CM-3.2 RULE-05. Emergency changes must complete testing and validation within 72 hours of implementation or be rolled back to maintain compliance.

scenarios.scenario-detail:
SecureDev Corporation, a defense contractor with 7,500 employees specializing in cybersecurity solutions for government agencies, operates advanced software development environments supporting classified and sensitive government projects across 18 secure facilities worldwide. The organization maintains comprehensive physical and environmental protection policies per PE-1 with designated officials, annual review cycles, complete policy dissemination, and full regulatory compliance with government security requirements and industry standards. Change management processes per CM-3.2 require comprehensive testing in production-equivalent environments, complete validation of security controls and functional requirements, detailed documentation with Change Control Board approval, and strict emergency change procedures with 72-hour post-implementation validation requirements. Privacy protection measures per PT-5.1 ensure just-in-time notices are presented at all PII collection points with appropriate timing, interface consistency, comprehensive logging of notice presentations, and regular updates when processing purposes change to maintain transparency with users. Supply chain risk assessments per RA-3.1 are conducted annually for all critical vendors with enhanced due diligence for high-risk suppliers, comprehensive evaluation of disruption risks, counterfeit components, malicious code insertion, and delivery practices, with documented integration into enterprise risk management processes and additional security validation for components from high-risk geographic regions. The authorized software policy per CM-7.5 implements comprehensive deny-all, permit-by-exception controls with quarterly software list reviews, detailed integrity verification using digital signatures and cryptographic checksums, comprehensive logging and monitoring of execution attempts, and monthly exception reviews with security team approval workflows and detailed documentation requirements. The development lifecycle incorporates comprehensive SA-10.5 requirements with real-time integrity mapping between master build data and on-site copies for all security-relevant components, automated cryptographic hash verification systems, comprehensive documentation of all verification activities with timestamps and responsible parties, and automated discrepancy detection mechanisms with 48-hour resolution requirements. However, following a critical security patch deployment to the main authentication system, the integrity verification between master build data and on-site copies was completed 30 hours after the version change due to resource constraints and competing priorities.

Policies referenced: policy_CM-7.5, policy_CM-3.2, policy_PT-5.1, policy_PE-1, policy_RA-3.1, policy_SA-10.5
scenarios.non-compliant-reason: The integrity verification was completed 30 hours after the version change, exceeding the required 24-hour timeframe specified in SA-10.5 RULE-02. The rule requires that integrity verification between master and on-site copies must be performed within 24 hours of any version change for security-relevant components.

scenarios.scenario-detail:
CyberGuard Financial Services, a major banking institution with 12,000+ employees across 45 branch locations, is implementing comprehensive security controls for their new digital banking platform processing customer financial data and regulatory compliance information. The Chief Security Officer deployed access control vestibules per PE-3.8 at all 8 designated high-security facilities including the primary data center, disaster recovery site, executive floors, security operations center, and research labs, with interlocking door systems preventing both doors from being open simultaneously for more than 12 seconds during normal operation and requiring individual authentication before granting second door access to prevent piggybacking attempts. The organization implemented baseline tailoring per PL-11 using SP 800-53B methodology for their FISMA Moderate baseline, documenting all tailoring actions with clear rationale linking to banking regulatory requirements, designating common controls for identity management and incident response across all systems, and selecting compensating controls for legacy mainframe systems that cannot implement modern authentication protocols while ensuring all control parameters are assigned specific organizational values based on comprehensive risk assessments. Network boundary protection devices follow SC-7.23 requirements with all firewalls, email gateways, and intrusion prevention systems configured to suppress detailed protocol validation error messages to external sources, using generic error responses for all protocol format validation failures, and ensuring debugging modes are disabled on all production boundary systems to prevent information disclosure to potential adversaries. The system architecture team implemented comprehensive partitioning per SC-32 with high-impact customer data systems residing in physically separate data centers with dedicated infrastructure, moderate-impact systems logically separated using network segmentation with managed interfaces restricting cross-domain communication through documented firewall rulesets, and all partitioning strategies documented in system security plans with detailed justification for separation decisions. Training feedback per AT-6 is provided quarterly to senior management including both general security awareness training results showing 94% completion rates and specialized role-based training for critical security positions, with personnel in critical roles who fail training reported to senior management within 3 business days and remedial training plans developed within 10 business days for any departments with completion rates below 85%. The organization maintains chain of custody per AU-10.3 for all information review and release activities with human reviewers uniquely identified and their current credentials associated with specific information processed, automated review functions properly authenticated using approved mechanisms, and complete documentation including reviewer identity, timestamps, information identifiers, and review purposes maintained throughout the information lifecycle.

Policies referenced: policy_PE-3.8, policy_PL-11, policy_SC-7.23, policy_SC-32, policy_AT-6, policy_AU-10.3

TechSecure Manufacturing Corporation, a defense contractor with 6,800+ employees supporting classified government contracts across 15 secure facilities, is deploying enhanced security controls for their new classified information processing system handling TOP SECRET materials. The Physical Security Manager implemented access control vestibules per PE-3.8 at all 12 high-security areas including classified processing facilities, executive briefing rooms, and secure development labs, with interlocking door systems configured to prevent simultaneous door opening for more than 10 seconds during normal operation, individual authentication required before second door access to eliminate piggybacking risks, and visual monitoring capabilities alerting security personnel within 25 seconds of any access violations while maintaining emergency egress capabilities during fire alarm conditions. The CISO applied baseline tailoring per PL-11 using approved SP 800-53B methodology for their FISMA High baseline, documenting comprehensive tailoring actions with detailed rationale linking to DoD security requirements and classified information handling needs, identifying common controls for physical security and personnel security across all classified systems, implementing compensating controls for air-gapped systems that cannot use network-based monitoring solutions, and assigning specific organizational parameter values based on threat assessments and operational security requirements. All network boundary protection systems comply with SC-7.23 requirements including perimeter firewalls, secure gateways, and network intrusion prevention systems configured to disable detailed protocol validation feedback to external sources, utilizing only generic error responses for protocol format validation failures from untrusted sources, and ensuring all debugging modes remain disabled on production boundary systems to prevent reconnaissance information disclosure to potential adversaries. System partitioning per SC-32 implements defense-in-depth strategies with TOP SECRET systems residing in physically separate facilities with dedicated infrastructure and geographical separation, SECRET systems logically partitioned using network segmentation with managed interfaces controlling information flow between security domains, and comprehensive documentation in system security plans detailing partitioning rationale and interface management controls. Training feedback per AT-6 provides quarterly reports to senior management covering security awareness training with 96% completion rates and specialized role-based training for security-cleared personnel, critical role training failures reported to senior management within 2 business days, and remedial training plans developed within 8 business days for any groups with completion rates below 85% to ensure continuous security competency. Chain of custody per AU-10.3 maintains complete accountability for all classified information review and release activities with human reviewers uniquely identified through security clearance validation, current credentials verified and associated with specific classified materials processed, automated review functions authenticated using approved cryptographic mechanisms, and comprehensive audit trails documenting reviewer identity, precise timestamps, classification levels, and detailed review purposes throughout the entire information lifecycle.

Policies referenced: policy_PE-3.8, policy_PL-11, policy_SC-7.23, policy_SC-32, policy_AT-6, policy_AU-10.3

HealthSecure Medical Systems, a healthcare organization with 9,200+ employees operating 28 medical facilities and processing protected health information (PHI) under HIPAA requirements, implemented comprehensive security controls for their new electronic health records system serving 450,000+ patients. The Chief Security Officer deployed access control vestibules per PE-3.8 at all 6 designated high-security facilities including the primary data center, medical records storage facility, executive administrative areas, and IT security operations center, with interlocking door systems preventing simultaneous door opening for more than 8 seconds during normal operation, individual biometric authentication required before second door access to prevent unauthorized following, visual monitoring systems alerting security personnel within 20 seconds of access violations, and emergency egress capabilities ensuring patient and staff safety during medical emergencies. Baseline tailoring per PL-11 utilized SP 800-53B methodology for their FISMA Moderate baseline customized for healthcare requirements, with all tailoring actions documented and justified based on HIPAA Security Rule compliance needs, patient safety requirements, and clinical workflow considerations, common controls designated for audit logging and access management across all PHI processing systems, compensating controls implemented for legacy medical devices that cannot support modern security protocols, and organizational parameter values assigned based on healthcare-specific risk assessments and regulatory guidance. Network boundary protection follows SC-7.23 requirements with medical network firewalls, healthcare application gateways, and network monitoring systems configured to suppress detailed protocol validation error messages to external sources, generic error responses implemented for all protocol format validation failures, and debugging modes disabled on all production healthcare boundary systems to prevent potential HIPAA violations through information disclosure. System partitioning per SC-32 implements healthcare-appropriate separation with high-impact PHI systems residing in physically separate secure data centers with dedicated medical-grade infrastructure, moderate-impact clinical systems logically partitioned using healthcare network segmentation with managed interfaces controlling patient data flow between clinical domains according to documented security policies, and comprehensive partitioning documentation in system security plans with detailed healthcare-specific justification for separation decisions and clinical workflow requirements. Training feedback per AT-6 provides quarterly comprehensive reports to senior management including HIPAA security awareness training results showing 97% completion rates and specialized role-based training for clinical and administrative staff handling PHI, critical healthcare role training failures reported to senior management within 4 business days to ensure patient data protection, and remedial training plans developed within 12 business days for any departments with completion rates below 85% to maintain HIPAA compliance standards. Chain of custody per AU-10.3 ensures complete accountability for all PHI review and release activities with healthcare personnel uniquely identified through credentialing verification, current medical staff privileges and security credentials associated with specific patient information accessed, automated clinical review functions authenticated using approved healthcare information exchange mechanisms, and detailed audit documentation including reviewer identity, precise timestamps, patient identifiers, and specific medical review purposes maintained throughout the entire PHI lifecycle to support HIPAA audit requirements.

Policies referenced: policy_PE-3.8, policy_PL-11, policy_SC-7.23, policy_SC-32, policy_AT-6, policy_AU-10.3

GlobalTech Energy Corporation, a critical infrastructure energy company with 7,500+ employees managing power generation and distribution systems across 22 facilities serving 2.8 million customers, implemented robust security controls for their new industrial control systems managing electrical grid operations under NERC CIP compliance requirements. The Physical Security Manager deployed access control vestibules per PE-3.8 at all 9 designated high-security facilities including the primary control center, backup dispatch facility, executive command center, cybersecurity operations center, and critical generation facilities, with interlocking door systems configured to prevent both doors from being open simultaneously for more than 14 seconds during normal operation, individual multi-factor authentication required before second door access to eliminate tailgating risks in critical infrastructure environments, comprehensive visual monitoring systems alerting security personnel within 28 seconds of any access violations, and emergency egress procedures ensuring rapid evacuation during power system emergencies while maintaining security protocols. Baseline tailoring per PL-11 applied SP 800-53B methodology for their FISMA High baseline customized for critical infrastructure protection, with comprehensive documentation of tailoring actions justified by NERC CIP requirements, North American Electric Reliability Corporation standards, and critical infrastructure protection needs, common controls designated for industrial network monitoring and incident response across all operational technology systems, compensating controls implemented for legacy SCADA systems that cannot support modern cybersecurity protocols without impacting power system reliability, and organizational parameter values specifically assigned based on critical infrastructure risk assessments and electrical grid operational requirements. Network boundary protection systems comply with SC-7.23 requirements including industrial firewalls, SCADA gateways, and operational technology network monitoring systems configured to disable detailed protocol validation feedback to external sources, generic error responses implemented for all industrial protocol format validation failures to prevent reconnaissance of critical infrastructure systems, and debugging modes permanently disabled on all production operational technology boundary systems to protect against potential grid cyber attacks. System partitioning per SC-32 implements critical infrastructure defense strategies with high-impact generation control systems residing in physically separate facilities with dedicated operational technology infrastructure and geographic distribution for grid reliability, moderate-impact distribution systems logically partitioned using industrial network segmentation with managed interfaces strictly controlling data flow between operational domains according to NERC CIP requirements, and comprehensive documentation in system security plans detailing partitioning rationale specific to electrical grid operations and critical infrastructure protection needs. Training feedback per AT-6 delivers quarterly detailed reports to senior management covering both general cybersecurity awareness training achieving 98% completion rates and specialized role-based training for critical infrastructure personnel including control room operators and cybersecurity staff, critical operational role training failures reported to senior management within 1 business day due to grid reliability implications, and remedial training plans developed within 7 business days for any operational groups with completion rates below 85% to ensure continuous protection of critical infrastructure systems. Chain of custody per AU-10.3 maintains rigorous accountability for all critical infrastructure information review and release activities with operational personnel uniquely identified through NERC CIP personnel risk assessments, current security clearances and operational credentials verified and associated with specific grid operational data accessed, automated industrial control review functions authenticated using approved operational technology security mechanisms, and comprehensive audit trails documenting reviewer identity, precise timestamps, system identifiers, and detailed operational review purposes maintained throughout the entire critical infrastructure information lifecycle to support NERC CIP compliance audits and grid security investigations.

Policies referenced: policy_PE-3.8, policy_PL-11, policy_SC-7.23, policy_SC-32, policy_AT-6, policy_AU-10.3

GlobalDefense Technologies, a major defense contractor with 15,000+ employees supporting classified TOP SECRET projects across 18 secure facilities, experienced a significant security policy violation when Senior Software Engineer Marcus Thompson accessed classified source code repositories outside authorized hours and downloaded sensitive cryptographic algorithms to unauthorized storage devices. The Chief Human Resources Officer immediately initiated the formal sanctions process per PS-8, conducting a thorough investigation with the Security Officer documenting violation details including system logs, access records, and witness statements from the security team who observed the unauthorized activity. The investigation revealed Thompson had violated multiple information security policies including data handling procedures, access control requirements, and acceptable use policies during the incident on March 15th. Legal Counsel reviewed the case and determined formal sanctions were warranted given the severity of accessing classified materials without proper authorization. The HR Security Team prepared comprehensive sanctions documentation identifying Thompson as the sanctioned individual, specifying the exact policies violated (IS-001 Data Handling Policy, AC-002 Access Control Policy, AU-003 Acceptable Use Policy), and detailing the reasoning for disciplinary action including potential security risk to classified programs. However, due to competing priorities and a major system outage requiring immediate attention, the designated security personnel and management team were not notified of the formal sanctions initiation until 36 hours after the decision was made. All other aspects of the sanctions process were properly executed including HR and Legal approval, complete documentation with evidence retention for the required seven-year period, and proportionate sanctions severity considering Thompson's clean disciplinary record. The organization maintained its documented formal sanctions process and ensured all access agreements and employment contracts included appropriate sanctions clauses as required.

Policies referenced: policy_PS-8, policy_SA-9.2, policy_SI-3.4, policy_SI-4.17, policy_PS-6.3, policy_SC-7.22
scenarios.non-compliant-reason: Violation of PS-8 RULE-03: When formal sanctions are initiated, designated personnel MUST be notified within 24 hours for security violations. The notification occurred at 36 hours, which exceeds the required 24-hour timeframe for security violations.

scenarios.scenario-detail:
CyberShield Corporation, a cybersecurity services company with 5,200+ employees providing managed security services to Fortune 500 clients, engaged CloudSecure Solutions as their primary external service provider for advanced threat detection and incident response capabilities across their global security operations centers. The Procurement Team worked diligently with CloudSecure to establish comprehensive service documentation per SA-9.2, obtaining detailed specifications of all security functions including threat hunting algorithms, behavioral analysis engines, machine learning detection models, and automated response capabilities. CloudSecure provided extensive documentation of specific port numbers including TCP 443 for HTTPS communications, TCP 22 for secure administrative access, UDP 514 for syslog data transmission, and TCP 1433 for database connectivity to their threat intelligence platform. The service provider also documented all data flows including encrypted threat telemetry streams, security event correlation processes, incident notification pathways, and compliance reporting mechanisms. However, during the documentation review process, CloudSecure's technical team failed to include specific protocol information in their service specifications, omitting details about TLS versions, encryption protocols, authentication mechanisms, and secure communication standards used for data transmission between CyberShield's infrastructure and CloudSecure's security platform. The Security Architecture Team reviewed the available documentation and noted the missing protocol specifications but approved the service implementation based on the comprehensive function and port documentation provided. All identification requirements were properly included in the external service contract and service level agreements, and CloudSecure committed to providing change notifications within the required 30-day timeframe for any future service modifications.

Policies referenced: policy_PS-8, policy_SA-9.2, policy_SI-3.4, policy_SI-4.17, policy_PS-6.3, policy_SC-7.22
scenarios.non-compliant-reason: Violation of SA-9.2 RULE-02: Service identification documentation MUST include specific port numbers, protocol types, data flows, and functional dependencies. The documentation was missing protocol types/specifications, making it incomplete despite having functions, ports, and data flows documented.

scenarios.scenario-detail:
SecureFinance Bank, a regional financial institution with 8,800+ employees managing critical banking operations across 45 branch locations, implemented comprehensive integrated situational awareness capabilities per SI-4.17 to protect against sophisticated multi-vector attacks targeting their financial systems and customer data. The Security Operations Center deployed an advanced correlation platform that successfully integrated data feeds from physical security systems including badge access controls, surveillance cameras, and environmental monitoring sensors, cybersecurity monitoring tools including SIEM platforms, network intrusion detection systems, and endpoint protection solutions, and supply chain risk monitoring systems tracking security posture of critical vendors and service providers. The correlation platform maintained sophisticated rules designed to detect coordinated attacks spanning physical breaches, cyber intrusions, and supply chain compromises, with automated analysis capabilities that could identify suspicious patterns across all operational domains. During a recent security incident, the correlation system detected simultaneous anomalies including an unauthorized physical access attempt at the main data center, unusual network traffic patterns indicating potential data exfiltration, and suspicious activity from a third-party payment processor that suggested possible compromise. The multi-domain correlation analysis was triggered at 2:15 PM when the first physical security alert was generated, followed by cyber anomaly detection at 2:18 PM and supply chain risk indicators at 2:22 PM. However, due to processing delays in the correlation engine and alert prioritization queues, the integrated situational awareness alert was not generated and delivered to the Security Operations Center until 2:40 PM, representing a 25-minute delay from the initial trigger event. The SOC team responded immediately upon receiving the correlation alert, conducting thorough investigation across all affected domains and implementing appropriate containment measures. All correlation rules were current, having been reviewed and updated the previous month, and supply chain monitoring data was properly maintained with weekly updates from all Tier 1 and critical Tier 2 suppliers.

Policies referenced: policy_PS-8, policy_SA-9.2, policy_SI-3.4, policy_SI-4.17, policy_PS-6.3, policy_SC-7.22
scenarios.non-compliant-reason: Violation of SI-4.17 RULE-02: Correlation analysis MUST be performed in real-time with alert generation within 15 minutes of detecting correlated events across multiple domains. The alert was generated at 25 minutes, which exceeds the required 15-minute timeframe.

scenarios.scenario-detail:
TechInnovate Solutions, a software development company with 3,400+ employees creating enterprise applications for healthcare and government clients, established comprehensive post-employment requirements per PS-6.3 to protect sensitive client information and proprietary development methodologies from unauthorized disclosure after employee termination. The HR Security Team developed detailed written notifications outlining legally binding post-employment obligations including non-disclosure agreements, non-compete clauses, intellectual property protection requirements, and data return obligations that all personnel must understand before accessing covered information systems. Legal Counsel thoroughly reviewed and approved all notification templates, ensuring compliance with applicable employment laws and enforceability of post-employment restrictions across all jurisdictions where the company operates. The organization implemented a robust process requiring all full-time employees, part-time staff, contractors, temporary personnel, and interns to receive written notification of these requirements as part of their initial access authorization to systems containing client data, source code repositories, and proprietary business information. For new software engineer Sarah Chen, the HR team properly provided the post-employment requirements notification during her onboarding process, explaining the legal obligations and business rationale for these protections. Chen received comprehensive information about her obligations regarding confidentiality, intellectual property rights, client data protection, and competitive restrictions that would continue after her employment ended. The notification clearly outlined specific requirements including immediate return of all company property, deletion of company information from personal devices, prohibition on soliciting company clients or employees, and restrictions on working for direct competitors for a specified period. However, during the final step of the access authorization process, Chen's manager inadvertently approved her system access to the main development environment and client data repositories before the signed acknowledgment form was received and processed by the HR Security Team. Chen began working on client projects and accessing covered information systems while the acknowledgment paperwork was still pending completion. The HR team discovered this oversight during their weekly access review process and immediately contacted Chen to complete the signed acknowledgment, which she provided within two business days of starting work.

Policies referenced: policy_PS-8, policy_SA-9.2, policy_SI-3.4, policy_SI-4.17, policy_PS-6.3, policy_SC-7.22
scenarios.non-compliant-reason: Violation of PS-6.3 RULE-02: Individuals MUST sign written acknowledgment of post-employment requirements as part of the access authorization process for covered information systems. Access was granted to covered systems before the signed acknowledgment was obtained, violating the requirement that acknowledgment must be part of the authorization process.
